title: Code Interpretation and Final Disposition
narrationScript: Everything in this course funnels into one moment. An indication is on the screen, characterized and located, and someone has to write accept or reject next to it. That word does not come from your judgment in the abstract. It comes from a document, the governing code, read correctly and applied to a correctly classified, correctly located discontinuity. Let's walk that path. First, which document governs? You never decide that at the part. The contract documents name the code, the edition, and any owner amendments. A pressure vessel is examined by the method rules of one article while the acceptance table lives in the referencing code section. A bridge or building weld answers to a structural welding code with its own ultrasonic tables tied to joint type and loading. In service equipment answers to in service rules, which are not the construction rules. The same indication can pass one document and fail another, so the first line of any evaluation names the code and the edition. Your written procedure is the bridge. It was prepared to satisfy that code, it was qualified and approved, and in the field you follow the procedure, not your memory of the code. If the procedure and the code ever seem to disagree, you stop and raise it. You do not freelance. Next, read the acceptance clause like a technician. Every clause has a scope, the joint types, thickness ranges, and weld categories it applies to, and applying a clause outside its scope is as wrong as misreading it. It defines a recording threshold, the level above which an indication must enter the report, commonly a percentage of the reference level. It gives evaluation rules, usually amplitude against the reference with length limits scaled to thickness, and often spacing rules for closely grouped indications. And nearly every construction code draws one bright line. Indications characterized as cracks, and usually lack of fusion and incomplete penetration as well, are rejectable regardless of amplitude or length. Mind the language. Shall is a requirement, should is advice, and the code's own definitions section settles what words mean. When wording is genuinely ambiguous, the remedy is a formal inquiry or a question up the chain to your Level Three and the responsible engineer, never a private interpretation recorded on a report form. That bright line about cracks is why classification matters so much, and amplitude cannot classify. You classify with echo dynamics and position together. A crack tends to give a sharp, ragged, multi peaked signal that keeps answering as you walk the probe along the weld, and it often shows from both sides. Lack of fusion gives a single sharp response standing exactly at the bevel angle. It collapses when you swivel the probe a few degrees and it favors one side of the weld strongly. Slag is more forgiving, a broader, rounder signal that survives probe rotation and lives in the weld volume. Porosity is a cluster of small traveling peaks, low amplitude grass. And geometry, root beads, backing bars, cap ripple, and mismatch, produces honest echoes from real features, which is why you plot every indication on a cross section of the joint before you condemn it, and confirm from the opposite side and a second angle whenever access allows. Location is trigonometry plus discipline. Soundpath times the sine of the refracted angle gives the surface distance from the index point. Soundpath times the cosine gives the depth on the first leg. Past the first leg, depth is twice the thickness minus soundpath times cosine. Run one with me. Sixty degree probe, twenty millimeter plate, reflector at fifty five millimeters of soundpath. One leg is forty millimeters, so we are in the second leg. Fifty five times the cosine of sixty degrees is twenty seven and a half. Forty minus twenty seven and a half puts the reflector twelve and a half millimeters below the scanning surface. Fifty five times the sine of sixty degrees puts it about forty seven and a half millimeters out from the index point. Plot that on the weld sketch and this one lands at the fusion line, which, together with the echo behavior, is your classification evidence. Finally, the report. Identification of the part, the weld, and the datum. Instrument, probe, wedge, calibration block serial number, and reference gain. For every recordable indication, the position along the weld, the surface distance, the depth and the leg, the amplitude against reference, the length from the six decibel drop, the classification, the exact clause applied, and the disposition. Rejected welds go to repair, and every repair is re examined with the same procedure and the same sensitivity, plus enough adjacent metal to prove the repair did not create a new problem. Sign it, date it, mark your level. A good report stands alone years later in front of an auditor or a failure board. Make the call from the document, show your arithmetic, and the call will stand.
imagePrompt: A Level II inspector plots a flaw location on a weld cross-section sketch on a clipboard while the flaw detector on the pipe shows a sharp echo, a thick code book open beside the angle-beam probe and scale on the workpiece.

sections.heading: Which Code Governs?
sections.body:
Disposition begins with a document, never with a preference. The **contract documents** — purchase order, design specification, owner standards — name the governing code, the **edition and addenda**, and any amendments. The distinctions matter:

- **New construction** pressure work typically takes its *method* from one article (calibration, scanning, recording rules in the style of ASME Section V, Article 4) and its *acceptance criteria* from the referencing construction code section.
- **Structural welds** answer to structural welding codes (AWS D1.1-style) whose UT acceptance tables key on joint class, thickness, and whether loading is static or cyclic.
- **In-service equipment** answers to in-service inspection rules, which are not the construction rules — the basis can shift from workmanship toward flaw evaluation.

The same indication can pass one document and fail another, so the evaluation's first line names code and edition. Your **written, approved procedure** is the bridge: prepared to satisfy that code, qualified, and binding — in the field you follow the procedure, not your recollection of the code. If procedure and code appear to conflict, stop and escalate; never freelance a fix on the report form.

sections.heading: Reading an Acceptance Clause
sections.body:
Acceptance clauses share an anatomy, and disciplined reading walks it in order:

- **Scope**: the joint types, thickness ranges, and examination categories the clause covers. Applying a clause outside its scope is as wrong as misreading it.
- **Recording threshold**: the level at which an indication must be recorded (commonly a percentage of the reference level/DAC) — recording is not rejection.
- **Evaluation rules**: typically amplitude versus reference combined with **length limits scaled to thickness**, plus proximity rules that force closely spaced indications to be treated as one.
- **Characterization triggers**: most construction codes make indications characterized as **cracks — and usually lack of fusion and incomplete penetration — rejectable regardless of amplitude or length**.

Read like an examiner: *shall* is a requirement, *should* is guidance; the code's own definitions section settles vocabulary. Genuine ambiguity is resolved by formal inquiry or escalation through your Level III and the responsible engineer — a private interpretation, however clever, is indefensible in audit and dangerous in service.

sections.title: Cracks are rejectable — full stop
sections.body: Under construction codes in the style of ASME Section VIII or ASME B31.1, indications **characterized as cracks, lack of fusion, or incomplete penetration are unacceptable regardless of amplitude or length**. Amplitude tables apply to the other discontinuity classes. This is why classification — not just measurement — decides dispositions, and why a wrong "slag" call on a real crack is the most expensive mistake in UT.

sections.heading: Classifying the Discontinuity: Echo Dynamics Plus Position
sections.body:
Amplitude cannot classify a reflector; **behavior and address** can. Work the probe and watch the signal:

- **Crack**: sharp, ragged, often multi-peaked envelope from facets; the response *persists as you walk the probe along the weld length*; frequently answers from both sides of the weld. Service cracks favor toes, roots, and repair zones.
- **Lack of fusion**: a single sharp peak standing precisely where a bevel-face reflector should be; strongly **one-sided**, and it *collapses with a few degrees of probe swivel* — a mirror you must face squarely.
- **Slag inclusion**: broader, rounder envelope that tolerates swivel and travel; lives in the weld volume between passes.
- **Porosity**: a cluster of low, narrow, traveling peaks — "grass" from many small spherical reflectors.
- **Geometry**: root bead, backing bar, cap ripple, and mismatch return honest echoes from real features at predictable addresses.

Discipline: **plot every indication on a joint cross-section** before condemning it, confirm from the opposite side and with a second angle when access allows, and let position (fusion line vs centerline vs root) corroborate the dynamic signature.

sections.caption: Echo-dynamic signatures that separate crack, lack of fusion, slag, and porosity when the probe is walked, swiveled, and swapped to the far side.
Field guide: classification evidence and its usual code consequence.
rows: Sharp, ragged, multi-peaked; persists along length; often both sides
Toe, root, repair zones; any orientation
Single sharp peak; dies on small swivel; one-sided
Rejectable regardless of amplitude (most construction codes)
Rounded, broader; survives swivel and travel
Stable echo at predictable position from a real feature
Not a flaw — verify by plotting and profile

sections.heading: Locating: The Trigonometry of Disposition
sections.body:
The acceptance clause is applied to a flaw at a **known address**, and the address comes from soundpath trigonometry (refracted angle θ, thickness T, soundpath SP):

- **Surface distance** from index point = SP·sin θ
- **Depth, first leg** = SP·cos θ
- **Depth, second leg** = 2T − SP·cos θ  (leg length = T/cos θ; skip distance = 2T·tan θ)

Worked example — 60° probe, T = 20 mm, SP = 55 mm: one leg is 20/cos 60° = 40 mm, so 55 mm is in the **second leg**. Depth = 2(20) − 55·cos 60° = 40 − 27.5 = **12.5 mm** below the scanning surface; surface distance = 55·sin 60° ≈ **47.6 mm**. Plotted on the joint sketch, that lands on the fusion line — which, with a sharp one-sided swivel-sensitive signal, completes a lack-of-fusion classification.

Position along the weld comes from the **datum system**: weld number, datum zero, measured distance, and side A/B convention. Length comes from the **6 dB-drop** endpoints for flaws larger than the beam. Sloppy addresses void good measurements: the repair crew excavates where the report says, not where the probe was.

sections.caption: Second-leg location of a fusion-line reflector with a 60-degree beam in 20 mm plate: depth 12.5 mm, surface distance about 47.6 mm from the index point.

sections.heading: The Final Report and the Repair Loop
sections.body:
The disposition lives or dies as a document. A complete final evaluation report carries:

- **Identification**: part, weld number, datum zero, drawing revision, material, thickness.
- **System**: instrument and probe identification, angle and frequency, wedge, couplant, calibration block serial number, procedure number and revision, **reference gain and DAC/TCG record**, code and edition applied.
- **Each recordable indication**: distance along weld from datum, surface distance, depth and leg, maximum amplitude versus reference, length (6 dB-drop endpoints), classification with its evidence, the **exact clause applied**, and the disposition.
- **Accountability**: examiner name, certification level, signature, date — and the reviewer where the program requires one.

Rejected welds enter the repair loop: excavate, repair, then **re-examine with the same procedure and sensitivity**, covering the repair plus enough adjacent metal to prove the excavation and re-weld created nothing new. The standard of quality for the report is blunt: years from now, in front of an auditor or a failure board, it must reconstruct the examination without you in the room — the call made from the document, with the arithmetic shown.

figures.title: Locating a reflector with a 60° angle beam

interactive.title: Capstone: Scan, Size, Locate, Disposition
interactive.intro: A 20 mm production butt weld, 60° shear-wave probe, DAC built from the basic calibration block, governing construction code named in the contract. An indication appears at 55 mm of soundpath, peaking at 80% of reference. Take it to disposition.

steps.prompt: First: where is the reflector? (60°, T = 20 mm, SP = 55 mm; cos 60° = 0.5)
options: 27.5 mm deep — below the back wall, so it is geometry
12.5 mm below the scanning surface, second leg (2×20 − 55×0.5)
7.5 mm below the scanning surface, first leg
20.0 mm deep, exactly at the back wall
steps.feedback: One leg is T/cos 60° = 40 mm, so 55 mm is in the second leg: depth = 2T − SP·cos θ = 40 − 27.5 = 12.5 mm, surface distance = 55·sin 60° ≈ 47.6 mm. Plot it: it lands on the fusion line.

steps.prompt: Working the probe: the signal is a single sharp peak, strongest from side A, standing exactly at the bevel-face position — and it collapses when you swivel the probe about five degrees. From side B it is barely there. Classification?
options: Porosity — a cluster of fine pores
Slag — volumetric, mid-volume
A planar fusion-face reflector — lack-of-fusion family
Root geometry — no record needed
steps.feedback: A mirror-like, one-sided, swivel-sensitive single peak sitting at the bevel angle is the textbook lack-of-fusion signature; slag would tolerate swivel, porosity would travel and stay low, and the plotted address is the fusion face, not the root.

steps.prompt: Amplitude is 80% of reference — below the 100% rejection line for amplitude-evaluated indications. The disposition for this indication under a typical construction code is:
options: Accept — it never reached reference level
Reject — indications characterized as lack of fusion (like cracks and incomplete penetration) are unacceptable regardless of amplitude
Accept if its length is under one-third of the thickness
Re-classify as slag so the amplitude table applies
steps.feedback: Characterization trumps amplitude: construction codes make crack, LOF, and IP rejectable at any amplitude. The amplitude tables exist for the other classes — and re-classifying to dodge a clause is falsification, not evaluation.

steps.prompt: Your draft report says: "LOF indication, weld W-12, reject." Before it is defensible, you must add:
options: Nothing — the disposition is stated
Location from datum with depth/leg and length, amplitude vs reference, instrument/probe/block IDs and reference gain, procedure and code clause applied, examiner name, level, and date
Only the repair welder's name
A photograph of the weld cap
steps.feedback: The repair crew excavates from your numbers and the auditor reconstructs your call from the record: full address, size, amplitude basis, equipment and calibration identity, the exact clause, and accountability. Then the repair is re-examined with the same procedure and sensitivity, plus adjacent metal.

checkQuestions.question: Who or what establishes which code edition governs the evaluation of a production weld?
options: The technician's preference for the latest edition
The contract documents (purchase order, design specification, owner amendments)
The instrument manufacturer's manual
Whichever edition gives the most conservative answer
checkQuestions.explanation: Code, edition, addenda, and amendments are fixed by the contract documents. Criteria change between editions, so naming the governing edition is the first line of a valid evaluation — not a field choice.

checkQuestions.question: For a 70° probe on 12 mm plate, the full skip distance (2T·tan θ, tan 70° ≈ 2.75) is about:
checkQuestions.explanation: Skip = 2T·tan θ = 2 × 12 × 2.75 ≈ 66 mm — the surface distance at which the beam returns to the scanning surface after one full V-path. Half that (33 mm) is the half-skip to the back wall.

checkQuestions.question: A single sharp echo stands exactly at the bevel-face position, responds strongly from one side of the weld only, and collapses when the probe is swiveled a few degrees. The most likely classification is:
options: Porosity
Lack of fusion on the bevel face
Slag inclusion
Cap ripple geometry
checkQuestions.explanation: A planar fusion-face reflector behaves like a mirror: it must be faced squarely (swivel kills it), favors the side that views the bevel face, and plots at the fusion line. Slag tolerates swivel; porosity is a low traveling cluster.

checkQuestions.question: With a 45° probe on 15 mm plate, an indication peaks at 33 mm soundpath (cos 45° ≈ 0.707). Its depth below the scanning surface is about:
options: 6.7 mm
checkQuestions.explanation: SP·cos θ = 33 × 0.707 ≈ 23.3 mm, which exceeds T = 15 mm, so the beam is in the second leg: depth = 2T − SP·cos θ = 30 − 23.3 ≈ 6.7 mm.

checkQuestions.question: After a rejected weld is repaired, the re-examination must be performed:
options: With the same procedure and sensitivity as the original examination, covering the repair and adjacent base metal
At reduced sensitivity, since the area was just inspected
By visual examination only
Only if the repair welder requests it
checkQuestions.explanation: Repairs are re-examined to the same procedure and calibration so results are comparable, and coverage includes adjacent metal to catch anything the excavation and re-welding introduced.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §6.2.3 (existing and applicable code interpretation)
CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §6.2.4 (type of discontinuity and location)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 4 with referencing-code acceptance criteria — cited generically
AWS D1.1-style structural UT acceptance tables (joint class, static vs cyclic) — cited generically
Angle-beam location trigonometry: surface distance = SP·sinθ; depth = SP·cosθ (1st leg) or 2T − SP·cosθ (2nd leg)
